title: Supporting someone in crisis
citation: Mind (2023). How to support someone in a mental health crisis. Mind UK.
content:
Watching someone you care about go through a mental health crisis is frightening. You may feel helpless, unsure what to say, or worried that you will make things worse. These feelings are understandable. The most important thing to know is that your presence and care genuinely matter, even when you feel like you are not doing enough.

If you are worried about someone, starting the conversation is usually the hardest part. A simple, direct question, such as asking how they are really doing, or saying that you have noticed they seem to be having a difficult time and that you are there to listen, is often enough to open a door. You do not need a script.

When someone is in distress, resist the urge to immediately offer solutions or reassurance. Listening without trying to fix is harder than it sounds and more valuable than most people expect. Reflect back what you hear. Acknowledge that things are hard. Let silences exist without rushing to fill them. The experience of being genuinely heard can itself be calming and connecting.

If the person is in immediate danger, your first priority is their safety. Stay with them, call for help if needed, and follow the guidance on what to do in a mental health emergency. Do not promise confidentiality if you believe someone is at risk.

After a crisis, staying in contact matters. People often feel shame or embarrassment after others have seen them at their most vulnerable. A simple message or a continued presence tells them that your care has not diminished.

Caring for someone in distress takes a toll. Your own wellbeing matters too. Seeking support for yourself, whether through a friend, a counsellor, or a support group for family members, is not selfish. It is what allows you to keep showing up.
